build-the-value-class#why-plus-equals-no.title: Why += and not =
build-the-value-class#why-plus-equals-no.hint: The diamond test fails with `=` because the second contribution overwrites the first. Any time a node has more than one outgoing edge, both paths' contributions must add — which is the chain rule's 'sum over paths' rule.
build-the-value-class#why-plus-equals-no.promptHTML: Pick the best explanation for why each _backward closure must use += instead of =.

options.html: It's a Python style preference — both work mathematically.
A node can be used multiple times. Each use deposits its own contribution to the parent's .grad. += accumulates them; = overwrites and loses all but the last.
+= is faster than = in Python's bytecode.
It's only needed when the graph has cycles.

joint-marginal-conditional#marginal-of-X.hint: Sum the joint over Y. P(X=1) = p(X=1, Y=0) + p(X=1, Y=1) = 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.5. Note: row-major means rows are X values.

p(0,0)=0.1,p(0,1)=0.4,p(1,0)=0.3,p(1,1)=0.2.p(0, 0) = 0.1, \quad p(0, 1) = 0.4, \quad p(1, 0) = 0.3, \quad p(1, 1) = 0.2.p(0,0)=0.1,p(0,1)=0.4,p(1,0)=0.3,p(1,1)=0.2.

joint-marginal-conditional#conditional-Y.title: Slice and rescale
joint-marginal-conditional#conditional-Y.hint: Slice the joint to the row where X = 1: cells (1,0)=0.3 and (1,1)=0.2. That row sums to 0.5 (the marginal P(X=1)). Now divide: P(Y=1|X=1) = 0.2 / 0.5 = 0.4.
joint-marginal-conditional#conditional-Y.promptHTML: Using the same joint from before (p(0,0)=0.1,p(0,1)=0.4,p(1,0)=0.3,p(1,1)=0.2p(0,0)=0.1, p(0,1)=0.4, p(1,0)=0.3, p(1,1)=0.2p(0,0)=0.1,p(0,1)=0.4,p(1,0)=0.3,p(1,1)=0.2) what is P(Y=1∣X=1)P(Y = 1 \mid X = 1)P(Y=1∣X=1)?

joint-marginal-conditional#bigram-chain-.hint: By the chain rule, P(·ab·) = P(a|·) · P(b|a) · P(·|b). Multiply the three given numbers: 0.3 · 0.4 · 0.2 = 0.024.
joint-marginal-conditional#bigram-chain-.promptHTML:

P(a∣⋅)=0.3,P(b∣a)=0.4,P(⋅∣b)=0.2.P(a \mid \cdot) = 0.3, \qquad P(b \mid a) = 0.4, \qquad P(\cdot \mid b) = 0.2.P(a∣⋅)=0.3,P(b∣a)=0.4,P(⋅∣b)=0.2.

linear-layers-collapse#two-layer-eval-at.title: Collapse it yourself
linear-layers-collapse#two-layer-eval-at.promptHTML:
A two-layer linear network (no biases, no activation) has

W(1)=(2003),W(2)=(11).W^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad W^{(2)} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}.W(1)=(20​03​),W(2)=(1​1​).

The network computes f(x)=W(2)(W(1)x)f(x) = W^{(2)}\bigl(W^{(1)}x\bigr)f(x)=W(2)(W(1)x).

What is f(x)f(x)f(x) for the input x=(1,1)x = (1, 1)x=(1,1)?

linear-layers-collapse#straight-boundary.title: Bug, or expected?
linear-layers-collapse#straight-boundary.hint: Linear layers compose into a single linear map. A linear map sends straight lines to straight lines, always. No bug — this is the math working exactly as proven above.
linear-layers-collapse#straight-boundary.promptHTML:
A learner builds a 5-layer network of linear layers with no activation functions, trains it on a dataset that needs a curved boundary, and notices the decision boundary is stubbornly a straight line.

Is this a bug, or expected behavior?

options.html: A bug — five layers should produce a complex boundary.
Expected — five linear layers collapse into one linear layer, which can only ever draw a straight boundary.
A bug — the learning rate is too low.
Expected — but only because the dataset is too small.

linear-layers-collapse#what-rescues-dept.title: What actually rescues depth?
linear-layers-collapse#what-rescues-dept.hint: The collapse happens for any linear (or, more generally, polynomial) map between layers. What breaks it is inserting a function that is not a polynomial — ReLU, sigmoid, tanh and GELU all qualify. ReLU is a common default, not the only option.
linear-layers-collapse#what-rescues-dept.promptHTML: The 5-layer network collapsed because every layer was linear. What is the smallest change that fixes it?

options.html: Use far more layers.
Use much larger weight matrices.
Insert a non-polynomial nonlinearity (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh, GELU, ...) between the layers.
Use the ReLU function specifically — nothing else can work.

P(a∣⋅)=0.13,P(d∣a)=0.05,P(a∣d)=0.50,P(⋅∣a)=0.20.P(a\mid\cdot) = 0.13,\quad P(d\mid a) = 0.05,\quad P(a\mid d) = 0.50,\quad P(\cdot\mid a) = 0.20.P(a∣⋅)=0.13,P(d∣a)=0.05,P(a∣d)=0.50,P(⋅∣a)=0.20.

loss-perplexity-and-how-to-sample#exp-lo.title: Loss to perplexity

loss-perplexity-and-how-to-sample#topk-r.title: Top-k by hand
loss-perplexity-and-how-to-sample#topk-r.hint: Top-2 keeps (0.5, 0.3). New normalizer = 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8. New P of top = 0.5 / 0.8 = 0.625.

now-make-it-talk#two-logit-softmax-tau-h.promptHTML:

now-make-it-talk#greedy-prob-mass-one.title: When sampling stops being random
now-make-it-talk#greedy-prob-mass-one.hint: Top-k=1 zeros out 64 of the 65 logits. After renormalization, all remaining probability is on the single surviving token. Temperature does not change this (it only rescales the relative ratios between the zeroed-out logits, which are already zero).
now-make-it-talk#greedy-prob-mass-one.promptHTML:

what-a-perceptron-really-is#preactivatio.title: Compute a pre-activation

A perceptron has weights w=(1,1)w = (1, 1)w=(1,1) and bias b=−1.5b = -1.5b=−1.5. You feed it the input x=(1,1)x = (1, 1)x=(1,1).

what-a-perceptron-really-is#bias-for-bou.title: Place the boundary
what-a-perceptron-really-is#bias-for-bou.hint: On the boundary, w·x + b = 0. With w = (1,1) and x = (0.5, 0.5): 0.5 + 0.5 + b = 0.
what-a-perceptron-really-is#bias-for-bou.promptHTML:
You keep the weights w=(1,1)w = (1, 1)w=(1,1) and want the decision boundary to pass exactly through the point (0.5, 0.5)(0.5,\ 0.5)(0.5, 0.5).

A point is on the boundary when w⋅x+b=0w \cdot x + b = 0w⋅x+b=0.

what-a-perceptron-really-is#classify-ori.hint: Compute z = 3·0 + (−2)·0 + 1 = 1. Then step(z): is z ≥ 0?
what-a-perceptron-really-is#classify-ori.promptHTML:
A perceptron has weights w=(3,−2)w = (3, -2)w=(3,−2) and bias b=1b = 1b=1. You feed it the input x=(0,0)x = (0, 0)x=(0,0).

what-a-radian-actually-is#full-revolutio.hint: The circumference of a circle is 2πr. Here r = 1, so one full lap has length 2π ≈ 6.2832.
what-a-radian-actually-is#full-revolutio.promptHTML:
You turn all the way around, one complete revolution, back to where you started.

The tip of the ray has walked the entire circumference of the unit circle. What arc length did it cover? (Give a decimal.)

what-a-radian-actually-is#convert-60-deg.title: Convert to radians

what-a-radian-actually-is#radian-from-ar.hint: On a circle of radius 1, the radian measure of an angle is defined to BE the arc length it sweeps. The arc is 3, so the angle is 3.
what-a-radian-actually-is#radian-from-ar.promptHTML:
On the unit circle, an angle sweeps out an arc of length 333.

What is the radian measure of that angle? (This is not a trick. Read the definition of a radian once more if you need to.)

what-approaching-actually-means#limit-of.title: Read the limit
what-approaching-actually-means#limit-of.hint: (x²−4)/(x−2) factors to x+2 for every x ≠ 2. As x closes in on 2, x+2 closes in on 4. The hole at x=2 is irrelevant.
what-approaching-actually-means#limit-of.promptHTML:
From the table, both sides of (x2−4)/(x−2)(x^2-4)/(x-2)(x2−4)/(x−2) close in on the same value as x→2x \to 2x→2.

What is lim⁡x→2x2−4x−2\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}\frac{x^2-4}{x-2}x→2lim​x−2x2−4​?

what-approaching-actually-means#sin-1-ov.title: Does it settle?
what-approaching-actually-means#sin-1-ov.hint: sin(1/x) swings between −1 and +1 infinitely often as x → 0. It never settles on one value, so the limit does not exist. Enter 0.
what-approaching-actually-means#sin-1-ov.promptHTML:
For lim⁡x→0sin⁡(1/x)\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\sin(1/x)x→0lim​sin(1/x), the table values keep bouncing between −1-1−1 and +1+1+1 no matter how far you zoom.

Does this limit exist? Enter 1 for yes, 0 for no.

what-approaching-actually-means#continuo.title: A plug-in limit
what-approaching-actually-means#continuo.hint: The function is continuous at x=1, so plug in: (ln 1 + e¹)/(1² + 3) = (0 + e)/4 = e/4 ≈ 2.71828/4 ≈ 0.6796.
what-approaching-actually-means#continuo.promptHTML:
The function ln⁡x+exx2+3\dfrac{\ln x + e^x}{x^2 + 3}x2+3lnx+ex​ is continuous at x=1x = 1x=1, so its limit there is just its value.

Compute lim⁡x→1ln⁡x+exx2+3\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1}\frac{\ln x + e^x}{x^2 + 3}x→1lim​x2+3lnx+ex​ as a decimal.

what-does-probability-measure#inclusion-.title: Add two events that overlap
what-does-probability-measure#inclusion-.hint: Inclusion-exclusion: $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$. The overlap got counted twice when you added the two pieces, so subtract it once.
what-does-probability-measure#inclusion-.promptHTML:
The third axiom only handles disjoint events. When the two events overlap, you have to subtract the double-count.

Given P(A)=0.6P(A) = 0.6P(A)=0.6, P(B)=0.5P(B) = 0.5P(B)=0.5, P(A∩B)=0.3P(A \cap B) = 0.3P(A∩B)=0.3, what is P(A∪B)P(A \cup B)P(A∪B)?

what-does-probability-measure#normalize-.title: Make a pdf valid
what-does-probability-measure#normalize-.hint: A valid pdf integrates to 1. So $\int_0^2 c x \,dx = 1$. The integral of $cx$ from 0 to 2 is $c \cdot 2^2 / 2 = 2c$. Set $2c = 1$.
what-does-probability-measure#normalize-.promptHTML:
Consider f(x)=cxf(x) = c xf(x)=cx on the interval [0,2][0, 2][0,2], and f(x)=0f(x) = 0f(x)=0 elsewhere. For this to be a valid pdf, the area under the curve has to equal 111.

what-is-a-derivative#secant-to-tangent-x.hint: Drag the h slider toward 0.01. Read the secant slope. Round to a whole number.
what-is-a-derivative#secant-to-tangent-x.promptHTML:

what-is-a-derivative#x-squared-slope-at-.hint: If you redid the slider trick at x=2 instead of x=1, you'd get 4. At x=3, you'd get 6. The pattern: f'(x) = 2x for f(x) = x².
what-is-a-derivative#x-squared-slope-at-.promptHTML:
You found f′(1)=2f'(1) = 2f′(1)=2 for f(x)=x2f(x) = x^2f(x)=x2 by sliding hhh to zero.

If you redid the trick at x=2x = 2x=2, you'd get 444. At x=3x = 3x=3, you'd get 666. There's a pattern, and you can probably already see it.

what-is-a-derivative#x-cubed-slope-at-2.title: One you haven't seen.
what-is-a-derivative#x-cubed-slope-at-2.hint: Same trick. Imagine sliding h to zero on f(x) = x³ at x = 2. Or guess the pattern from f'(x) = 2x for x²: maybe f'(x) for x³ is 3x²?
what-is-a-derivative#x-cubed-slope-at-2.promptHTML:
